title: Daily Protein Intake Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate your daily protein needs in grams by weight, goal, and age. Based on RDA 0.8 g/kg up to 2.4 g/kg for muscle gain or cutting.
intro: Getting enough protein is the single most evidence-backed lever for body composition, recovery, and healthy aging. This calculator applies **peer-reviewed ranges** — from the conservative RDA of **0.8 g/kg** for sedentary adults up to **2.4 g/kg** for those lifting while in a calorie deficit — and adjusts upward by **20 % for adults over 65**, who need more protein to counter age-related muscle loss (sarcopenia).
keyTakeaway: A 180 lb (82 kg) active adult needs roughly 98–131 g of protein per day — equivalent to about 3–4 chicken breasts or 14–19 large eggs.
useCases: Setting a daily protein target before starting a strength-training program
Adjusting intake during a calorie-deficit (cutting) phase to preserve lean mass
Checking whether an older adult's diet meets the higher protein needs that offset sarcopenia
Planning meals with specific protein sources (chicken, eggs, tofu) to hit a goal
Comparing protein requirements between a sedentary lifestyle and an active one
Consulting with a dietitian — use the result as a starting reference point

options.label: Cut (Lose Fat) — lifting in a calorie deficit

explanation:
## How It Works

This calculator converts your body weight to kilograms, selects an evidence-based protein range for your goal, and applies a **+20 % senior adjustment** if you are 65 or older.

## Formula

```
weight_kg = weight_lb / 2.2046   (if entered in lb)

protein_min_g = weight_kg × rate_low  × senior_factor
protein_max_g = weight_kg × rate_high × senior_factor

senior_factor = 1.20  (age ≥ 65)  |  1.00  (age < 65)
```

### Rate Table by Goal

| Goal | Low (g/kg) | High (g/kg) | Evidence basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sedentary | 0.8 | 0.8 | US/Canadian RDA (DRI 2005) |
| Active | 1.2 | 1.6 | ISSN Position Stand 2017 |
| Build Muscle | 1.6 | 2.2 | Morton et al. 2018 meta-analysis |
| Cut (deficit + lifting) | 1.8 | 2.4 | Helms et al. 2014; ISSN 2017 |

### Senior Adjustment (+20 %)

Adults aged 65+ experience **anabolic resistance**: muscle protein synthesis responds less efficiently to a given dose of protein. The European Society for Clinical Nutrition (ESPEN) recommends 1.0–1.2 g/kg as a minimum for healthy older adults, representing roughly a 25–50 % increase over the standard RDA. This calculator applies a conservative +20 % multiplier across all goals for this age group.

## Worked Example

**Profile:** 180 lb, Active, Age 40

1. weight_kg = 180 / 2.2046 = **81.6 kg**
2. Rate range (active) = 1.2 – 1.6 g/kg
3. senior_factor = 1.00 (age < 65)
4. protein_min = 81.6 × 1.2 × 1.00 = **97.9 g/day**
5. protein_max = 81.6 × 1.6 × 1.00 = **130.6 g/day**

**Food equivalents (midpoint ≈ 114 g):**
- ~3.8 chicken breasts (4 oz cooked each = ~30 g protein)
- ~18 large eggs (~6.3 g protein each)
- ~5.5 servings firm tofu (½ cup = ~20 g protein)

## Limitations

- **Body composition not considered.** Ranges are based on total body weight. Lean-mass-based calculations (using body fat %) may yield different results.
- **Kidney disease.** High-protein diets are contraindicated for individuals with chronic kidney disease (CKD). Consult a physician before exceeding 0.8 g/kg if you have CKD.
- **Pregnancy / lactation.** Protein needs increase significantly; this calculator does not cover those scenarios.
- **Ranges, not absolutes.** Research shows wide individual variation. The ranges here reflect population-level evidence, not personalized metabolic assessment.

faq.q: What is the RDA for protein, and is it enough for active people?
faq.a: The US RDA is 0.8 g/kg of body weight per day — the minimum to prevent deficiency in sedentary adults. For people who exercise regularly, research consistently shows 1.2–2.2 g/kg is optimal for performance and body composition. The RDA is a floor, not a target for active individuals.

faq.q: Why does the calculator give a range instead of a single number?
faq.a: Protein needs vary with training intensity, calorie intake, age, and individual response. Ranges reflect real research variability. Aim for the lower bound on lighter training days and the upper bound when training volume or deficit is high.

faq.q: Should I use total body weight or lean mass for the calculation?
faq.a: This calculator uses total body weight, which is the most practical input. If your body fat is above ~30 %, lean-mass-based calculations (multiply fat-free mass by 1.6–2.2 g/kg) may be more accurate and will typically yield lower absolute targets.

faq.q: Why is the protein target higher for cutting (fat loss) than for building muscle?
faq.a: In a calorie deficit, the body is at greater risk of burning muscle for energy. Higher protein intake (1.8–2.4 g/kg) combined with resistance training is the most effective strategy to preserve lean mass while losing fat, per Helms et al. (2014) and the ISSN Position Stand.

faq.q: Why do adults over 65 need more protein?
faq.a: Older adults experience anabolic resistance — muscles respond less efficiently to protein. ESPEN guidelines recommend ≥1.0–1.2 g/kg/day as a baseline for healthy seniors, rising to 1.2–1.5 g/kg with illness or activity. A +20 % adjustment captures this conservatively across all goals.

faq.q: Is it safe to eat 2.0–2.4 g/kg of protein per day?
faq.a: For healthy adults with normal kidney function, research up to 3.0 g/kg/day has not shown adverse effects (Antonio et al., 2016). However, individuals with chronic kidney disease (CKD) should not exceed 0.8 g/kg without medical supervision, as excess protein increases kidney filtration load.

faq.q: How much protein is in common foods?
faq.a: A 4 oz (113 g) cooked chicken breast provides ~30 g; one large egg ~6.3 g; ½ cup (126 g) firm tofu ~20 g; one cup (245 g) Greek yogurt ~17–20 g; 3 oz (85 g) canned tuna ~22 g. These figures come from the USDA FoodData Central database.

faq.q: Does protein timing matter — should I spread it evenly across meals?
faq.a: Yes. Research suggests muscle protein synthesis is maximized by consuming 20–40 g of high-quality protein per meal, spread across 3–5 meals. Eating your entire daily target in one sitting is less effective than distributing it throughout the day.

faq.q: Do plant-based protein sources count the same as animal protein?
faq.a: Plant proteins are often lower in one or more essential amino acids and may have lower digestibility (PDCAAS/DIAAS scores). Vegans and vegetarians should aim for the upper end of each range and combine complementary sources (e.g., rice + beans, tofu + edamame) to ensure a complete amino acid profile.

faq.q: How accurate are the food-equivalent estimates in the results?
faq.a: The estimates use standard USDA reference values: chicken breast 30 g/4 oz cooked, large egg 6.3 g, firm tofu 20 g per ½ cup. Actual protein content varies by brand, cooking method, and portion size. Use a nutrition label or app for precision meal planning.

sources.name: A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains in muscle mass and strength (Morton et al.)
ESPEN Guidelines on Clinical Nutrition and Hydration in Geriatrics

dataUpdate.notes: Update rate ranges if major systematic reviews or DRI revisions change evidence-based recommendations. USDA food protein values are stable but should be verified against FoodData Central on major revisions.
